title: Tapping for Never Enough Hours
subtitle: An EFT sequence for chronic time pressure and the exhaustion of always running behind
excerpt: An EFT tapping sequence for the feeling that there is never enough time: the perpetual list, the day that ends before the important things, the exhaustion of always being behind. Taps through the time panic and toward a different relationship with the hours that actually exist.
sourceNotes: Written for homemade.education. EFT framework developed by Gary Craig (mid-1990s).

practice.whenToUse: Use when the feeling of time poverty is loud — at the start of a day that already feels impossible, or when the panic of never-enough is running.

content.text: An EFT tapping sequence for the chronic feeling that there isn't enough time. The feeling is real, and the time pressure is often real. But the anxiety that sits on top of the real time pressure tends to make the problem worse, not better. This sequence works on the anxiety layer. Not to pretend the hours are there, but to stop the panic from eating the ones that are.
New to tapping? Read
how tapping works
first.
The practice
Karate chop
Tap the karate chop point and say each statement once:
Even though there is never enough time and I'm exhausted by it, I deeply and completely accept myself.
Even though the day feels impossible before it starts and that feeling is real, I accept that this is where I am right now.
Even though I'm running behind on my own life and don't know how to fix that, I am willing to sit with this for a few minutes.
Tapping round
Tap each point seven times while saying the phrase:
Eyebrow: There is never enough time.
Side of Eye: The list is always longer than the day.
Under Eye: Everyone else wants a piece of it before I do.
Under Nose: The day is gone before I've done what matters.
Chin: The rush that doesn't stop even when the day does.
Collarbone: I'm behind on my own life.
Under Arm: I don't know how to fix this and I'm tired of trying.
Top of Head: The panic that makes it all worse.
Reframe
Eyebrow: There are a fixed number of hours, and the panic doesn't add more.
Side of Eye: Some things on the list can wait. My body knows which ones.
Under Eye: Other people's demands on my time are not all equally urgent.
Under Nose: What matters most today is usually smaller than the full list.
Chin: I can release the rush without adding to the problem.
Collarbone: One anchor point today is enough.
Under Arm: The panic is the problem I can do something about right now.
Top of Head: I have more hours than the panic lets me see.
Tap each round three times, then move to the reframe and tap that three times.
Where this practice comes from
Written for homemade.education. The EFT framework is Gary Craig's Emotional Freedom Technique, in use since the mid-1990s.
